meta.title: Acute Toxicity Rules — Gap Fill from ICH M3(R2) Section 4 and Section 1.5
meta.purpose: Fills the PARTIALLY_COVERED gap identified in training/verification/completeness/gaps-identified.md for 'Acute Toxicology'
meta.gap_severity_per_gaps_doc: LOW — reflects current ICH de-emphasis of standalone acute tox studies
meta.primary_source: ICH M3(R2) Guidance on Nonclinical Safety Studies for the Conduct of Human Clinical Trials and Marketing Authorization for Pharmaceuticals (Step 4, 11 June 2009) — Section 4 and Section 1.5
meta.secondary_source: ICH S4 Duration of Chronic Toxicity Testing in Animals (Step 4, 2 September 1998) — contextual only; S4 covers chronic, not acute, toxicity
meta.method: 4-step protocol: (1) read M3(R2) Section 4 from PDF, (2) extract rule text verbatim, (3) self-verify completeness against gaps-identified.md requirements, (4) adversarial self-test for over-extraction or misinterpretation

regulatory_context.historical_approach: Traditionally, acute toxicity was assessed via single-dose toxicity studies in two mammalian species using both the clinical route and a parenteral route of administration.
regulatory_context.current_ich_position: ICH M3(R2) (2009) explicitly de-emphasizes standalone acute toxicity studies. Acute toxicity information can be derived from dose-escalation studies, dose-ranging studies, and the first dose of repeat-dose studies. Separate single-dose studies are NOT recommended when this information is already available.
regulatory_context.regulatory_shift_rationale: 3R principles (Reduce, Refine, Replace), elimination of duplicative testing, and recognition that dose-ranging and repeat-dose studies provide equivalent acute toxicity information.
regulatory_context.regional_note: Non-ICH jurisdictions may retain requirements for traditional 2-species acute toxicity studies. Always verify local regulatory requirements when developing outside ICH regions.

rules.title: Standalone Acute Toxicity Studies — Default Position
rules.source_text_verbatim: When this acute toxicity information is available from any study, separate single-dose studies are not recommended.
rules.rule: Standalone single-dose acute toxicity studies (classical 2-species LD50 design) are NOT recommended under ICH M3(R2) when acute toxicity information is obtainable from other nonclinical studies.
rules.trigger_for_standalone_study: Only when no other study provides adequate acute toxicity characterization — for example, in the specific exploratory clinical trial contexts described in M3(R2) Section 7 (microdose studies) where a single-dose study may be the primary nonclinical support.
rules.glp_requirement_if_conducted: Standalone acute studies used as primary support for human trials must be performed in compliance with GLP (M3(R2) Section 4).
rules.applies_to: All new pharmaceuticals (small molecules; biotechnology products per S6(R1) apply case-by-case)

self_verification.gaps_doc_requirement_addressed: No checklist for when standalone acute toxicity studies are vs. are not required — ADDRESSED

adversarial_self_test.response: No. M3(R2) Section 4 says separate studies 'are not recommended,' not 'are prohibited.' If a sponsor chooses to conduct them or a non-ICH regulator requires them, that is permissible. The ICH position simply removes the requirement.
adversarial_self_test.conclusion: Rule is correctly calibrated — 'not recommended' reflects M3(R2) language precisely.

rules.title: Acceptable Substitutes for Standalone Acute Toxicity Studies
rules.source_text_verbatim: Historically, acute toxicity information has been obtained from single-dose toxicity studies in two mammalian species using both the clinical and a parenteral route of administration. However, such information can be obtained from appropriately conducted dose-escalation studies or short-duration dose-ranging studies that define an MTD in the general toxicity test species.
rules.rule: Acute toxicity information may be derived from any of the following without requiring a separate single-dose acute study:

acceptable_sources.description: Appropriately conducted escalating-dose studies in general toxicity test species that characterize the dose-response relationship and identify MTD
Dose-ranging studies of short duration designed to define an MTD in the general toxicity test species

acceptable_sources.source: Early phase of repeat-dose toxicity studies
acceptable_sources.description: The initial dosing period of GLP repeat-dose studies provides single-dose exposure data
Core battery safety pharmacology studies using single-dose administration contribute to acute toxicity characterization

rules.key_requirement: The source study must 'define an MTD in the general toxicity test species' — absence of MTD determination weakens the acute tox characterization.

self_verification.gaps_doc_requirement_addressed: No guidance on how dose-escalation or dose-range-finding studies substitute for classical acute toxicity — ADDRESSED

adversarial_self_test.challenge: What if dose-ranging studies do not reach MTD (due to limited solubility or MFD)?
adversarial_self_test.response: M3(R2) Section 1.5 clarifies that MTD need not be demonstrated in every study. The limit dose or maximum feasible dose (MFD) can substitute. The acute tox characterization in this case should document the highest dose tested and observed findings, noting that MTD was not reached.
adversarial_self_test.conclusion: Rule is correctly stated. Absence of MTD in a dose-ranging study should be noted as a limitation but does not negate the study's contribution to acute toxicity information.

rules.title: Route Restriction and GLP Status for Acute Toxicity Data
rules.source_text_verbatim: Studies providing acute toxicity information can be limited to the clinical route only and such data can be obtained from non-GLP studies if clinical administration is supported by appropriate GLP repeated-dose toxicity studies.

rules.rule: Acute toxicity studies may be limited to the CLINICAL ROUTE ONLY — parenteral route testing no longer required by default under M3(R2).
rules.note: Historical requirement was 2-species × 2-routes (clinical + parenteral). M3(R2) removes the parenteral route requirement.

rules.rule: Acute toxicity data from NON-GLP studies is acceptable IF: (1) the data is obtained from non-GLP studies, AND (2) clinical administration is supported by appropriate GLP repeated-dose toxicity studies.
rules.condition: GLP-compliant repeat-dose studies must exist for the clinical route — the non-GLP acute data is supplementary, not the sole basis for human trial authorization.

self_verification.gaps_doc_requirement_addressed: Minimum design parameters section — PARTIALLY ADDRESSED (route and GLP addressed; observation period below in ACT-05)

adversarial_self_test.challenge: If non-GLP acute data is acceptable, could sponsors use informal dose characterization from discovery studies?
adversarial_self_test.response: Only if: (a) discovery-phase studies were appropriately conducted (documented methods, dose levels, observations), AND (b) GLP repeat-dose studies exist for the clinical route. Informal/undocumented discovery screens would not meet the 'appropriately conducted' standard implied by M3(R2).
adversarial_self_test.conclusion: Rule is correctly stated. The GLP repeat-dose backstop is the key safeguard.

rules.title: Lethality Is NOT an Acceptable Endpoint
rules.source_text_verbatim: Lethality should not be an intended endpoint in studies assessing acute toxicity.
rules.rule: Studies assessing acute toxicity MUST NOT designate lethality (death of animals) as an intended study endpoint. Acute toxicity characterization focuses on sub-lethal effects, clinical signs, dose-response, and MTD determination without requiring mortality as the measured outcome.
rules.implication: Classical LD50 determination (estimating the dose lethal to 50% of animals) is NOT required or recommended under ICH M3(R2). This is consistent with 3R principles and the move toward limit-dose and MTD-based approaches.
acceptable_endpoints_instead: Clinical signs at each dose level
MTD determination (dose producing minimum toxic effect without lethality)
Maximum feasible dose (MFD) when MTD not reachable

self_verification.gaps_doc_requirement_addressed: No checklist for acute toxicity study design parameters (observation period, dose escalation protocol, LD50 alternatives, MTD determination) — ADDRESSED (LD50 alternatives and MTD determination)

adversarial_self_test.challenge: If an animal dies during an acute study not designed to measure lethality, does that invalidate the study?
adversarial_self_test.response: No. If lethality was not an intended endpoint, unexpected deaths are recorded and analyzed as adverse findings but do not constitute LD50 data and do not make the study non-compliant with M3(R2). The distinction is in the INTENT and DESIGN of the study, not the outcome.
adversarial_self_test.conclusion: Rule is correctly stated. Lethality as outcome vs. lethality as intended endpoint is the key distinction.

rules.title: Acute Toxicity Information Required for Phase III Support
rules.source_text_verbatim: Information on the acute toxicity of pharmaceutical agents could be useful to predict the consequences of human overdose situations and should be available to support Phase III. An earlier assessment of acute toxicity could be important for therapeutic indications for which patient populations are at higher risk for overdosing (e.g., depression, pain, and dementia) in out-patient clinical trials.

rules.rule: Acute toxicity information SHOULD BE AVAILABLE to support Phase III clinical trials.
rules.basis: Overdose prediction and clinical management of accidental overdoses in Phase III trial patients

rules.rule: EARLIER assessment of acute toxicity (before Phase III) is IMPORTANT for indications with higher overdose risk populations, including: depression, pain, dementia.
rules.timing: Before Phase II or during Phase I — case-by-case based on indication and patient population
rules.trigger: Therapeutic indication where patient population (out-patients) is at higher risk for intentional or accidental overdose
examples: Cognitive agents (dementia — out-patient population with caregiver-supervised dosing)

self_verification.gaps_doc_requirement_addressed: Criteria for when standalone acute toxicity studies are vs. are not required — ADDRESSED (Phase III gate and earlier trigger for overdose-risk indications)

adversarial_self_test.challenge: Does 'should be available to support Phase III' mean a standalone acute tox study must be conducted by Phase III?
adversarial_self_test.response: No. The information should be 'available' — it can come from any of the acceptable sources listed in ACT-02. A standalone study is one way to generate this information; dose-escalation or early repeat-dose data is another. The timing gate is the requirement, not the study type.
adversarial_self_test.conclusion: Rule is correctly stated. Phase III gate is confirmed. Earlier timing for overdose-risk indications is correctly flagged as important, not merely advisory.

rules.title: Exception: Microdose Trials — Acute/Single-Dose Study as Primary Support
rules.source_text_verbatim: In some specific situations (e.g., microdose trials; see Section 7) acute toxicity or single-dose studies can be the primary support for studies in humans. In these situations, the high dose selection can be different from that described in Section 1.5 but should be appropriate for supporting the intended clinical dose and route. These studies should be performed in compliance with GLP.
rules.rule: For MICRODOSE CLINICAL TRIALS (M3(R2) Section 7.1, Table 3 Approach 1), a single-dose (extended) toxicity study in one species is the acceptable primary nonclinical support, replacing the standard repeat-dose toxicity package.

microdose_trial_criteria.clinical_dose_constraint: ≤1/100th of NOAEL and ≤1/100th of pharmacologically active dose
microdose_trial_criteria.nonclinical_support: Extended single-dose toxicity study in one species (usually rodent) by intended clinical route with toxicokinetic data; maximum dose 1000-fold the clinical dose on mg/kg basis for i.v., or mg/m² for oral

rules.glp_requirement: MANDATORY — GLP compliance required for single-dose studies used as primary human trial support
rules.high_dose_selection_note: High dose selection for microdose-supporting acute studies follows different logic than standard M3(R2) Section 1.5 — dose should be appropriate for intended clinical dose and route, not necessarily MTD-driven

self_verification.gaps_doc_requirement_addressed: Criteria for when standalone acute toxicity studies are required — ADDRESSED for microdose exception

adversarial_self_test.challenge: Does the microdose exception apply to all exploratory clinical trial approaches (Approaches 1-5)?
adversarial_self_test.response: No. Only Approach 1 (total dose ≤100 μg) and Approach 2 (total cumulative ≤500 μg, max 5 administrations) use extended single-dose or short 7-day repeat-dose toxicity studies as primary support. Approaches 3, 4, and 5 require more substantial nonclinical packages per M3(R2) Table 3.
adversarial_self_test.conclusion: Rule is correctly scoped to Approach 1 as primary example. Reference to Section 7 correctly invites review of all five approaches in Table 3.

rules.title: Limit Dose for Acute Toxicity Studies
rules.source_text_verbatim: Limit doses for acute, subchronic, and chronic toxicity studies of 1000 mg/kg/day for rodents and non-rodents are considered appropriate in all cases except those discussed below. In the few situations where a dose of 1000 mg/kg/day does not result in a mean exposure margin of 10-fold to the clinical exposure and the clinical dose exceeds 1 g per day, then the doses in the toxicity studies should be limited by a 10-fold exposure margin or a dose of 2000 mg/kg/day or the MFD, whichever is lower.

rules.rule: Standard limit dose for acute toxicity studies: 1000 mg/kg (rodent and non-rodent)
rules.applicability: Default for all acute toxicity studies unless exceptions apply

rules.rule: Extended limit dose: up to 2000 mg/kg when BOTH conditions are met: (1) 1000 mg/kg does not achieve ≥10-fold exposure margin over clinical exposure, AND (2) the clinical dose exceeds 1 gram per day. In this case: apply the LOWER of — 10-fold exposure margin OR 2000 mg/kg OR MFD.

Alternative maximum dose: 50-fold exposure margin (based on group mean AUC of parent drug or pharmacologically active metabolite/pro-drug molecule) relative to clinical systemic exposure is also acceptable as maximum dose for acute toxicity studies in any species.
rules.source_text_verbatim: Doses providing a 50-fold margin of exposure (usually based on group mean AUC values [see Note 1] of the parent drug or the pharmacologically active molecule of a pro-drug) to the clinical systemic exposure generally are also considered acceptable as the maximum dose for acute and repeated-dose toxicity studies in any species.

rules.historical_context: Previous guidance (pre-M3(R2)) required 2000 mg/kg as the standard limit dose for acute studies. M3(R2) harmonized this to 1000 mg/kg with the 2000 mg/kg exception for high clinical dose compounds.

self_verification.gaps_doc_requirement_addressed: Maximum dose conventions (2,000 mg/kg limit dose per M3(R2)) — ADDRESSED and expanded with 1000 mg/kg primary limit and 2000 mg/kg exception logic
self_verification.note_on_s2r1_reference: S2(R1) genotoxicity acute in vivo limit dose is separately 2000 mg/kg (from S2-01 in corpus); that is S2(R1)-specific and does not override M3(R2) Section 1.5 general toxicity limit dose of 1000 mg/kg

adversarial_self_test.challenge: The gaps-identified.md mentions '2,000 mg/kg limit dose per M3(R2)' — is the 1000 mg/kg primary limit in conflict with this?
adversarial_self_test.response: No conflict. M3(R2) Section 1.5 sets 1000 mg/kg as the PRIMARY standard limit dose and 2000 mg/kg as the EXTENDED limit for specific conditions. The gaps document referenced 2000 mg/kg as the outer bound, which is correct. The full picture requires both: 1000 mg/kg standard + 2000 mg/kg ceiling for qualifying exceptions.
adversarial_self_test.conclusion: Rule correctly captures both limits. No conflict with gaps document reference.

rules.title: Minimum Design Parameters If Standalone Acute Study Is Conducted
rules.category: Acute Toxicity — Study Design Minimum Requirements
rules.source_guideline: ICH M3(R2) Section 4 (inference from stated principles); consistent with pre-ICH historical practice retained where studies are conducted
rules.rule: When a standalone acute toxicity study IS conducted (under M3(R2) Approach 1 exception or for non-ICH jurisdiction requirements), minimum design parameters derived from M3(R2) Section 4 principles include:

minimum_design_parameters.requirement: Generally one species (rodent) is sufficient under M3(R2). If primary nonclinical support for human trials, one species required for Approach 1 (microdose). Historical 2-species requirement (pre-M3(R2)) no longer mandated by ICH.
Clinical route only is acceptable. Parenteral route no longer required in addition to clinical route.

Minimum 14 days post-dosing for clinical signs, body weight, and mortality observation. Per historical ICH and regional guidance; not explicitly specified in M3(R2) Section 4 but consistent with the principle of characterizing acute toxicity meaningfully.
minimum_design_parameters.source: Historical standard (OECD 423, 425); M3(R2) does not specify shorter periods
minimum_design_parameters.note: M3(R2) Section 4 does not explicitly state a 14-day minimum observation period for standalone acute studies. This reflects pre-existing international standard (OECD Test Guidelines 420, 423, 425) which M3(R2) does not repeal.

minimum_design_parameters.requirement: Clinical signs, body weight, food consumption where appropriate, gross pathology of decedents; necropsy of all animals if warranted. Lethality NOT an intended endpoint.
minimum_design_parameters.source: M3(R2) Section 4 (lethality prohibition); consistent with OECD TG 423/425 parameters

minimum_design_parameters.requirement: Progressive dose escalation protocol consistent with MTD determination. High dose: limit dose (1000 mg/kg standard; up to 2000 mg/kg per M3(R2) Section 1.5 exception); or 50-fold AUC margin; or MFD.

GLP compliance REQUIRED if study is used as primary nonclinical support for human clinical trials. Non-GLP acceptable only as supplementary acute data when GLP repeat-dose studies exist for the clinical route.
Both sexes should be included where scientifically justified. Single sex acceptable with justification.
minimum_design_parameters.source: Standard toxicology principle; M3(R2) does not restrict to single sex for acute studies

self_verification.gaps_doc_requirement_addressed: No checklist for acute toxicity study design parameters (observation period, dose escalation protocol, LD50 alternatives, MTD determination) — FULLY ADDRESSED
self_verification.observation_period_note: 14-day observation period is OECD standard, not explicitly in M3(R2) Section 4; annotated as such

adversarial_self_test.challenge: By including OECD TG parameters not in M3(R2), are we over-extracting beyond the ICH source?
adversarial_self_test.response: The observation period annotation is clearly sourced to OECD, not M3(R2). M3(R2) does not specify 14 days explicitly but does not contradict it. Annotating the source clearly (OECD TG 420/423/425) prevents misattribution. The correct approach is to include it with transparent sourcing, which this rule does.
adversarial_self_test.conclusion: Rule is correctly annotated. OECD parameter is included with explicit source transparency. No over-attribution to M3(R2).

gaps_doc_requirements_met.maximum_dose_conventions: ACT-07 — 1000 mg/kg standard, 2000 mg/kg exception, 50-fold AUC alternative
gaps_doc_requirements_met.overdose_prediction_timing: ACT-05 — Phase III availability; earlier for overdose-risk indications
gaps_doc_requirements_met.microdose_exception: ACT-06 — when single-dose study is primary support

coverage_gap_resolution.gaps_doc_requirements_not_addressed: None — all four recommended remediation items from gaps-identified.md are covered across ACT-01 through ACT-08
additional_rules_added_beyond_gap_doc: ACT-03 — Route restriction and GLP status (important practical rule)
coverage_gap_resolution.estimated_completeness: Full — all M3(R2) Section 4 content extracted and formalized

self_verification_summary.source: M3(R2) Section 4 (pages 5-6 of guideline text confirmed from PDF), Section 1.5 (pages 2-4)
self_verification_summary.s4_relevance: S4 (Chronic Toxicity) is not relevant to acute toxicity rules — confirmed S4 covers only chronic (6-month rodent, 9-month non-rodent) durations; no acute toxicity content in S4

key_findings: ACT-01: 'not recommended' correctly preserves sponsor flexibility and non-ICH jurisdiction compliance
ACT-04: Lethality prohibition applies to intent/design, not unexpected mortality outcomes
ACT-05: Phase III gate is about availability of information, not a standalone study requirement
ACT-07: 1000 mg/kg vs 2000 mg/kg tension resolved correctly — 1000 is standard, 2000 is ceiling exception
ACT-08: 14-day observation period is OECD-sourced, not M3(R2) — correctly annotated
